4:T691,(must_read: detailed technical analysis)
1) Unification via Bregman Divergence Flow Matching (BDFM). The authors observe embedding-space DLMs naturally align with ODE/flow formulations and connect them to Flow Matching. They further justify the standard cross-entropy objective by relating it to minimizing a specific Bregman divergence under the flow-matching view, yielding a principled and simplified training objective compared to prior multi-objective or batch-slicing recipes.
2) Evaluation: a novel ODE-based NLL upper bound. To enable principled PPL evaluation for continuous flow-based LMs, they derive an ODE-based upper bound on negative log-likelihood, argued to be better suited than prior SDE/ELBO-style bounds for their ODE sampling setup.
3) Noise scheduling: the information-uniform principle + learnable scheduler. They argue language embeddings require a different noise schedule than images. Using the log noise-to-signal ratio γ as the time-conditioning variable, they propose distributing “information” uniformly over time. Empirically, they find an optimal γ schedule resembling a Gumbel distribution and introduce a learnable Gumbel-based noise scheduler.
4) Training protocol: revisiting self-conditioning for continuous DLMs. They highlight that self-conditioning behaves differently in discrete vs continuous diffusion and revise the training recipe accordingly, improving both likelihood and sample quality.
(skim: method overview) LangFlow = embedding-space continuous diffusion/flow LM + cross-entropy justified via Bregman-FM + ODE-based NLL/PPL bound + information-uniform γ-time noise schedule (Gumbel) + improved self-conditioning training.5:T4d7,(must_read: deep reading)
- Start with the contribution summary at the end of the Introduction to map the three pillars: BDFM training, ODE-NLL evaluation, and schedule/self-conditioning design.
- Focus sections:
  1) §3.1 (embedding-space DLM ↔ Flow Matching ↔ Bregman divergence): verify how cross-entropy is derived/justified as a Bregman-FM objective.
  2) §3.2 (ODE-based NLL bound): check assumptions, tightness sources, how it differs from SDE/ELBO bounds, and how PPL is computed in practice.
  3) §4.1 (information-uniform principle, γ conditioning, Gumbel schedule): understand why language differs from images; inspect the profiling evidence that leads to the Gumbel conclusion.
  4) §4.2 (self-conditioning revision): compare with discrete diffusion self-conditioning and identify what changes for continuous embedding-space diffusion.
- Figures/tables to prioritize: schedule profiling and performance curves; ODE-NLL vs SDE-bound comparison; self-conditioning ablations.
(skim: fast reading)
- Read Introduction + contributions.
- Skim §3.2 for the PPL computation recipe.
- Skim §4.1 for the key empirical takeaway (γ + Gumbel schedule).
- Read main result tables for LM1B/OWT PPL and zero-shot transfer summary.6:T691,(must_read: detailed technical analysis)
1) Metric/diagnosis: introspective acceptance rate measures whether the model “accepts” its previously generated tokens when re-evaluated—used to expose inconsistency in DLMs relative to AR.
2) Key insight: AR’s advantage is structural. Causal masking + logit shifting not only enables next-token prediction but also enforces a form of self-verification/consistency over past tokens. Standard DLM denoising objectives lack this closed loop.
3) I-DLM training: introspective-consistency training converts a pretrained AR model into a DLM with relatively small additional training (the paper mentions ~5B tokens), without distillation schedules or masking curricula. The core is to explicitly enforce agreement with a causal anchor distribution while enabling parallel generation.
4) Decoding: Introspective Strided Decoding (ISD) generates N tokens per forward pass while simultaneously verifying/revising prior tokens at designated introspection positions, using the causal anchor as reference. The authors claim the resulting outputs provably match the base AR distribution, without confidence heuristics or separate verification passes.
5) Systems co-design: an AR-compatible serving stack (e.g., SGLang-compatible). A gated residual adaptation mechanism applies LoRA only at mask positions while verification uses base weights, enabling a continuum from near-lossless to strictly lossless modes.
(skim: method overview) I-DLM injects AR-like self-agreement into DLM training; ISD unifies generation and verification in one pass; system design reuses AR serving patterns and uses gated LoRA for efficiency/losslessness trade-offs.7:T4d6,(must_read: deep reading)
- Read §1–§2 and Figures 1/2 to align the two threads: quality gap and systems mismatch.
- Focus sections:
  1) Formal definition and computation of introspective acceptance rate, and evidence linking it to generation quality/consistency.
  2) Introspective-consistency training: objective formulation, which positions are masked vs introspected, how the causal anchor is used, and why small continued training suffices.
  3) ISD algorithm: token layout per stride, how generation+verification are combined in one forward pass, and the conditions/proof sketch for matching the base AR distribution.
  4) Gated residual adaptation (LoRA gating): why enabling adapters only at mask positions yields an efficiency/losslessness trade-off relevant for deployment.
- Figures/tables to prioritize: acceptance-rate plots; stride vs quality/speed curves; serving throughput/latency/cost comparisons against AR and prior DLMs.
(skim: fast reading)
- Read the Introduction for the core insight and contributions.
- Skim the ISD algorithm figure/description to understand what happens per forward pass.
- Jump to the main benchmark and systems tables to validate the “AR-level quality + real efficiency gains” claim.8:T76c,[must_read: detailed technical analysis]
1) Main change (Muon2): apply Adam-style adaptive second-moment, element-wise scaling to the momentum matrix before the NS-based orthogonalization. Concretely: form momentum M_t, maintain a second-moment accumulator V_t (EMA of squared signals), compute a preconditioned momentum \tilde{M}_t = M_t / (\sqrt{V_t}+\epsilon) element-wise, then run K NS iterations on \tilde{M}_t to obtain an approximate polar/orthogonal factor Q_t, and update parameters with an additional \sqrt{m/n} learning-rate scaling.
2) Key insight: the practical difficulty in Muon’s polar approximation is not the need for exact orthogonality. The original Muon already tolerates fairly inexact orthogonalization (e.g., mapping singular values only roughly into [1-ε,1+ε] with ε up to ~0.3). The real issue is that the input momentum matrix can be ill-conditioned with an unfavorable singular-value spectrum, slowing NS convergence. Muon2’s second-moment preconditioning empirically improves the spectrum/conditioning, which:
- speeds up NS convergence to a practically sufficient orthogonalization;
- improves directional alignment of the finite-iteration update with the “true” orthogonalized direction.
3) Metric shift: the paper argues that orthogonality error metrics (e.g., \|Q^TQ-I\|) alone do not capture what matters for optimization; directional alignment and finite-iteration utility are more relevant.
4) Muon2-F: a memory-efficient variant using a factorized second-moment preconditioner to avoid storing full second-moment tensors, retaining most of Muon2’s gains under large-scale memory constraints.

[skim: method overview]
Insert an Adam-like second-moment, per-parameter scaling right before Muon’s NS orthogonalization, making the matrix easier to orthogonalize so fewer NS steps are needed; provide a factorized-second-moment option to reduce memory.9:T434,Triadic Suffix Tokenization (TST): (1) Integer part: group digits right-to-left into 3-digit triads (base-1000) and append an explicit magnitude suffix per triad (k/m/b/t/q…), yielding a deterministic 1:1 mapping between suffix and order of magnitude; (2) Fractional part: group digits left-to-right into triads and mark depth with replicated p markers (p, pp, ppp…), while right-padding each fractional triad with zeros to length 3 to enforce a canonical form so 0.1, 0.10, and 0.100 map to the same token sequence; (3) Two implementation options: Option A uses separate tokens for triads and markers (~10 new tokens, longer sequences and potential binding ambiguity); Option B uses compound tokens like “234m” (~10k new tokens total for integer+fractional depths, shorter sequences and unambiguous binding); (4) Suffix vs prefix placement: for digit-level tokenization, suffixes provide deterministic triad boundaries, align with human notation, and implicitly encode remaining length; for compound tokens, prefix/suffix is largely immaterial at the embedding level.a:T4f6,The paper introduces a second temperature, position temperature T_pos, which controls stochasticity in *which positions* get committed next, orthogonal to token temperature T_token that controls entropy over *which token* is sampled at a position.

Two tempered remasking heuristics are proposed:
1) TLC (Tempered Low-Confidence): a stochastic relaxation of low-confidence remasking. Instead of greedily selecting the lowest-confidence positions, positions are sampled according to a tempered low-confidence distribution, preventing perpetual deferral of uncertain (often fork) tokens.
2) TCT (Tempered Confidence Thresholding): a tempered version of confidence-threshold decoding. It retains adaptive speed (variable number of tokens committed per step) while softening the commit/selection decision via T_pos to improve exploration.

Theory: the authors define fork tokens operationally (high-entropy, branch-determining positions) and analyze an idealized fork model. For TLC, increasing T_pos is shown to provably increase expected entropy at forks, explaining why tempering the position order restores diversity.

Practically, changes are localized to the position-selection rule, keeping computational benefits and implementation simplicity of existing heuristics.b:T426,must_read (deep dive):
- Key sections:
  1) Section 3.2 for exact TLC/TCT definitions and how to implement them.
  2) Section 3.1 for the fork-token formalization and the proof that higher T_pos increases expected fork entropy (mechanistic explanation).
  3) Section 4.1 for the main pass@k and cost-normalized comparisons (especially Figures 2 and 3).
  4) Section 4.3 for the GRPO rollout study (practical downstream impact).
- Key figures: Figure 2 (pass@k scaling vs AR), Figure 3 (compute-aware scaling).
- Innovation checklist:
  (i) identifies greediness in remasking—not parallel decoding—as the diversity bottleneck;
  (ii) introduces T_pos as an orthogonal control knob to T_token;
  (iii) links position-order stochasticity to exploration via fork-entropy analysis.

- Inference-compute dynamics: compare quasi-autoregressive vs non-sequential decoding as steps increase, revealing saturation vs continued refinement.

skim (quick read):
- Read the Introduction for framing;
- Skim Methodology for the two control groups and task definitions;
- Jump to Table 1 and its discussion for the main conclusion;
- Briefly scan the failure-mode list to build an intuition for diffusion-specific risks.e:T57c,The paper analyzes confidence-based fully-NAR decoding and identifies two intrinsic issues plus a temporal asymmetry, then proposes two minimal sampling-order interventions:
1) Proximity Bias: during denoising from an all-[MASK] state, high-confidence unmasking decisions concentrate on spatially adjacent token positions. This localizes the denoising order, causing spatial error propagation: early locally plausible but globally wrong fragments get locked in and spread to neighbors.
2) Premature EOS dominance: EOS can become high-confidence early and gets unmasked too soon, effectively shrinking the available generation window and harming tasks requiring longer reasoning trajectories.
3) Temporal asymmetry of importance: the first denoising step’s position selection (which positions to unmask) disproportionately shapes and often locks the entire trajectory.
4) Interventions (sampling-time only, no diffusion-backbone fine-tuning):
  - A lightweight planner trained to predict an optimal set of initial positions to unmask, anchoring the trajectory toward the target sequence.
  - EOS temperature annealing that lowers EOS unmasking priority in early steps (then relaxes later), preventing premature truncation.
Notably, even with standard greedy token selection, these position-level early interventions yield large gains and generalize plug-and-play to higher-step (higher-compute) regimes.f:T4b6,must_read (deep technical + what to focus on)
- Introduction: extract the full causal chain—proximity bias → spatial error propagation; premature EOS → window collapse; and the “first-step matters most” temporal asymmetry.
- Figure 1: the key visual argument contrasting (a) unconstrained confidence-based NAR dynamics vs (b) early trajectory shaping (planner + EOS annealing).
- Method details on position selection/unmasking order: the main novelty is guiding “where to unmask first,” not changing the diffusion backbone.
- Empirical analysis section on proximity bias: how it is measured (spatial clustering over time) and linked to failures.
- EOS temperature annealing: understand it as priority modulation (not banning EOS), balancing length control and reasoning space.
- Ablations: planner vs no planner, first-step-only vs more steps, compatibility with greedy/other token selection.

skim (high-level + what to read)
- Read Introduction + Figure 1 only for the problem-mechanism-solution loop.
- Skim Preliminaries just to recall MDLM iterative unmasking.
- Jump to experimental conclusions: what the failure modes are and how much the two interventions help across compute regimes.10:T40b,Two main components:
1) Cross-Lingual Mapping (CL) Task for continued pre-training: jointly optimize standard Next-Token Prediction (NTP) and a CL objective that performs bi-directional language mapping in the model’s embedding space, encouraging direct cross-lingual correspondences while retaining monolingual fluency via NTP. The key design choice is to make alignment an intrinsic pre-training objective rather than a downstream-only alignment step.
2) Language Alignment Coefficient (LAC): to overcome the unreliability of mean cosine similarity across layers, LAC is defined as the mean cosine similarity of cross-lingual sentence-pair embeddings divided by the standard deviation of these similarities over a selected layer subset (statistically equivalent to inverse coefficient of variation). Embeddings are taken from the last token hidden states at layers in L_sub (e.g., {5,10,15,20,25}). Higher LAC indicates stronger and more stable alignment across layers, improving robustness to outliers and small-sample evaluation.11:T43b,Setup: frozen-probe on Pythia-160M (12 layers) and Pythia-410M (24 layers), trained on WikiText-103. All pretrained weights are frozen; only injected modules (pre-proj, skip, or LoRA) are trained. Benchmarks: LAMBADA, HellaSwag, ARC-Easy, and WikiText-103 perplexity. Training: 500 steps, AdamW + cosine, LR 1e-5, effective batch 16, bf16; evaluation on 500-sample subsets.
Key findings:
- 160M: pre-proj+skip is best overall—LAMBADA 0.180 (+40.6%), HellaSwag 0.338 (+3.7%, the only method improving HellaSwag), PPL 47.9 (~-38.9%). LoRA mainly improves PPL but can hurt LAMBADA; pre-proj alone boosts LAMBADA more than PPL.
- 410M: pre-proj+skip achieves best PPL (17.0), beating even a much larger LoRA setup (94.4M params, PPL 17.7). LAMBADA 0.484 is slightly below LoRA 0.488 but with fewer added parameters.
- Interpretability (Fig. 2): skip strength (weight norm) consistently increases in later layers across scales, concentrated in the final 25% of layers, supporting the view that early layers benefit from positional routing while later layers benefit from content bypass.12:T791,INCRT is an incremental grow/prune/homeostasis loop driven by an online spectral residual.
1) Geometry and residual: For head h, define M^h=W_Q^h(W_K^h)^T and its antisymmetric “motor” M_a^h=(M^h-(M^h)^T)/2∈so(d) capturing directional flow. At layer l, collect K orthonormal directions covered by active heads into Q^(l)∈R^{d×K}, and form the residual projector P_⊥=I−QQ^T.
2) Residual matrix: In feature space, use the Gram matrix X^T X (explicitly not XX^T), combine it with the mean antisymmetric motor \bar{M_a}, and symmetrize via sym(·) to ensure real eigenvalues, yielding a symmetric residual matrix A_res.
3) Scalar deficit and threshold: Define residual directional energy Γ_res=||A_res||_F. INCRT has a single free hyperparameter, the growth threshold θ_w>0; if Γ_res>θ_w the current architecture is insufficient and triggers growth. A pruning threshold φ_g∈(0,θ_w) is derived from θ_w.
4) Growth direction: Use the top eigenpair (λ_max(A_res), v_1(A_res)) to identify the most deficient direction; add a new head in the direction that most reduces this measured deficit.
5) Multi-scale incremental design: The architecture grows at three nested scales (C1) and uses a bidirectional PCA+MCA gate with almost-sure convergence (Theorem 1) to stably track/update the directional subspace.
6) Criterion equivalence: The paper proves an equivalence among geometric, NTK, and practical growth criteria with an explicit non-tuned constant (Theorem 3, Corollary 4).
7) Main theory: Theorem 6 (homeostatic convergence) shows INCRT reaches a finite-step stopping configuration where no further admissible growth or pruning can occur, and this configuration is simultaneously minimal and sufficient w.r.t. the residual directional criterion. Theorem 7 provides a compressed-sensing-style upper bound on the number of heads, scaling with the square of the task spectral condition number times a logarithmic factor.13:T483,must_read (deep)
- Introduction: redundancy diagnosis (symmetric vs antisymmetric roles) and the “single scalar spectral trigger” narrative.
- Section 3 (Architecture): focus on how A_res is constructed (why X^T X, why sym), the definition of sufficiency via Γ_res≤θ_w, and the three nested growth scales (C1).
- Section 4 (Theory):
  - Theorem 6: finite-step stopping + minimal-and-sufficient configuration; understand how growth/pruning events are ruled out.
  - Theorem 7: head-count upper bound and its dependence on spectral condition number (assumptions and the log factor).
  - Theorem 1 (PCA+MCA gate convergence) and Theorem 3/Corollary 4 (three-criterion equivalence) as the key “non-heuristic” pillars.
- Section 5 (Experiments): prioritize plots/tables aligning predicted vs observed head counts and sensitivity to θ_w.
skim (quick)
- Read Introduction + the core residual spectral criterion and grow/prune procedure in Section 3.
- Jump to Section 5 main result figures (head count, performance, convergence behavior).
- From theory, only keep Theorem 6 (it stops and is minimal/sufficient) and Theorem 7 (head count is bounded).14:T6a4,TA reframes retention from statistical salience to structure-prior utility boosting.
1) Setup: For context tokens x1…xn, a retention policy π selects a subset S⊆{1…n}, |S|≤K after each step. Each token i gets a utility u_i; top-u_i tokens are retained.
2) Why baselines fail: A dormant token has cumulative attention A_i<ε before the query, so any attention- or reconstruction-driven scoring ranks it near the bottom.
3) Key observation: Dormant-but-critical values follow predictable structural patterns; anchor tokens (e.g., “key:”, “Authorization:”) reliably precede value spans.
4) Anchor detection: three instantiations—(i) regex pattern set; (ii) semantic pattern extension (e.g., “said/told/remember”); (iii) embedding-based detection via cosine similarity to anchor concept embeddings (language-agnostic).
5) Utility scoring: TA defines a composite u_i combining cumulative attention A_i, recency R_i=i/n, anchor indicator S_i, a pension score (EMA of past attention), frequency penalty F_i, and a sponsorship voucher V_i; the paper provides fixed weights (α=1.0, β=0.5, γ=0.3, δ=0.2, λ=0.1).
6) Voucher allocation: when an anchor at position i is detected, it “sponsors” the next L tokens with exponentially decaying protection, controlled by sponsor budget B (defaults: B=15, L=6). This injects a proactive retention signal into u_i, making sponsored value tokens survive eviction even if their attention is ~0.
7) TA-Fast: an attention-free variant reducing overhead and remaining compatible with efficient attention kernels; TA modifies the scoring function rather than replacing the underlying eviction policy, enabling composition with existing methods.15:T62c,Key findings and explanations:
1) Empirical result: at matched storage, quantization consistently outperforms rank reduction across all tested settings. INT4 is near-lossless, while equal-budget rank reduction can severely degrade PPL and even collapse downstream accuracy. Hybrids (rank reduction + quantization) still fail to match full-rank INT4.
2) Mechanism (theory + geometry): attention weights α=softmax(QK^T/√d) implement routing with discrete decision boundaries, especially sensitive when the top-2 score gap Δ is small.
- Rank reduction deletes directions, removing their contribution to Δ and easily crossing boundaries, flipping which token is attended (a discrete failure).
- Quantization adds bounded noise; for b≥4, perturbations typically do not cross boundaries, so routing is mostly preserved.
This is formalized via a softmax Fisher-metric perturbation asymmetry, yielding an upper-bound ratio where projection damage can exceed quantization damage by a factor scaling with 2^{2b}. Under aggressive GQA, a single deleted direction can flip routing for multiple query heads, amplifying the gap.
3) Downstream-optimal projection (Theorem 1): for a linear component W feeding differentiable f, the best rank-r subspace (first-order) comes from the top eigenvectors of M=E[W^T J_f^T J_f W · xx^T]. For attention values, f(·)=α·(·) is linear, giving an exact basis. This unifies PCA, Fisher-SVD, GPTQ, AWQ, KQ-SVD, and can recover part of rank-reduction damage (reported 52% gap recovery at zero cost), but does not overturn quantization’s dominance.16:T4c9,must_read (deep technical reading)
- Introduction: focus on the pivot argument—why eviction-centric scorer refinement is insufficient, and why role differentiation (core vs scratch) matters; treat “keep-set churn is limited” as the thesis.
- Method: extract (i) how the core is defined/identified and protected, (ii) how selective consolidation differs from eviction, and (iii) how the two-stage design prevents prompt-heavy blockage.
- Results: read in the authors’ intended evidence chain (H100 gate → prompt-heavy replay → actual-output bridge) and map outcomes to the “usable budget frontier” claim.
- Appendix A/B: A for Phase-1 failure/pivot rationale; B for the horizon-kernel / score-family mathematical assets used for diagnostics.
- Key figures/tables to locate: keep-set churn comparisons, budget–behavior frontier curves, prompt-heavy two-stage ablations, and representative-set statistics.

skim (fast understanding)
- Read the first half of the Introduction + contributions list to capture the reframing.
- Skim the Method diagram/pipeline: core protection + scratch consolidation + two-stage.
- Check only the headline result tables/curves showing full-KV behavior retention at lower budgets.17:T47a,1) Full-stack root-cause analysis across three layers:
- Framework: operator dispatch (e.g., PyTorch SDPA) may fall back from optimized implementations (FlashAttention) to naive/eager paths when shapes violate constraints.
- Library: cuBLAS/cuBLASLt/Triton choose different kernels depending on dimensions; misalignment can force slower kernels or extra overhead.
- Hardware: Tensor Cores impose alignment/tile constraints (commonly 8-alignment); violating them reduces throughput.
2) GAC (GPU-Aligned Compression):
- Input: an upstream compressor’s continuous/irregular per-layer dimension allocations derived from importance scores.
- Goal: under the same parameter budget B, re-select discrete per-layer dimensions that satisfy alignment constraints (e.g., d mod 8 = 0) while preserving the compressor’s intended budget distribution.
- Formulation/solver: cast dimension selection as a constrained multi-choice knapsack and solve via dynamic programming to guarantee both budget and alignment.
Key property: compressor-agnostic—GAC does not change the upstream scoring/compression logic; it fixes the deployment-facing dimension choices.18:T513,must_read (deep technical reading)
- §2 Motivation: internalize the phenomenon with concrete stats/cases (e.g., Llama-3-8B + ASVD/PaLU) and the misalignment definition.
- §3 Full-Stack Analysis: the most valuable section—follow the chain framework dispatch → library kernel selection → hardware alignment, and map each to its triggering conditions and measured evidence.
- §4 GAC: understand the constrained optimization (multi-choice knapsack) and why DP provides budget/alignment guarantees; focus on how it wraps arbitrary compressors.
- §5 Evaluation: verify end-to-end latency gains, operator-level breakdowns, and alignment ratio changes; ensure gains are attributable to alignment.
- Key figures/tables: GEMM dimension schematic (Fig. 1), misalignment distributions (Fig. 2/3), root-cause overview (Fig. 4), latency penalty vs alignment (Fig. 5), and the main speedup table.

skim (high-level overview + what to read)
- Read the end of Introduction + Fig.1/2/3 to quickly grasp: why attention-based pruning is biased, why SVD helps, and where the method sits.
- Skim the equations: remember F, SVD, leverage via U row norms, select top-K and keep order.1a:T4b8,POINTS-Long introduces a native dual-mode MLLM inspired by human vision:
- Focus Mode: processes the full visual sequence, preserving the original model’s fine-grained capability (Principle P1).
- Standby Mode: uses a drastically reduced set of “Standby tokens” to approximate the full sequence for holistic long-video perception (P2).
Design principles emphasize deployment simplicity and compatibility with modern inference frameworks (P3) and extensibility to existing MLLMs (P4).
It is enabled via a two-stage post-training pipeline:
1) Visual Distillation & Alignment: freeze the original MLLM and train a small set of newly added modules between the vision backbone and the projector to distill information from full Focus tokens into compact Standby tokens, keeping the Focus pathway unaffected.
2) LLM Mode Adaptation: lightly fine-tune the LLM (small learning rate, high-quality data) so it can interpret inputs from both modes.
A detachable KV cache mechanism supports streaming by combining Focus as a high-fidelity “present” buffer and Standby as a compressed “short-term” cache, reducing costly context re-prefills. The system is evaluated with SGLang to ensure practical speedups.1b:T4d8,must_read (deep technical analysis + what to focus on)
- Introduction: the “three production blockers” and principles P1–P4 define the paper’s constraints and evaluation lens.
- Method overview: how Focus/Standby are defined and how mode switching is intended to work.
- Read the referenced Sec.3.2/3.3 carefully:
  1) Visual distillation: how Standby tokens are produced, objectives/losses, alignment, and why Focus remains unaffected.
  2) LLM mode adaptation: why a second stage is needed, training strategy, and how forgetting is avoided.
- Detachable KV cache section/figures: understand the streaming memory design (present vs short-term cache) and how it reduces re-prefill.
- Experiments: token reduction vs accuracy retention on OpenCompass, throughput gains (6.2×), and evidence that Focus mode preserves original fine-grained performance.

skim (high-level overview + what to read)

  - ensure comparisons are under strictly equal memory budgets,
  - look for ablations (protection only vs allocation only vs both),
  - separate impacts on localization metrics vs generation metrics.
- Deployment angle: verify runtime overhead and whether CPG extraction is offline; check robustness across repos/languages.1d:T44b,CodeQuant is a unified clustering-and-quantization framework for low-precision MoE models (three stages in Figure 1).
Stage 1: Activation-oriented Outlier Smoothing (AOS)
- Apply a rotation matrix R to input activations (leveraging rotation invariance in both self-attention and MoE FFNs), effectively suppressing activation outliers by relocating them into weight space.
- To avoid online overhead, share the same R across the router and all experts.
- Fine-tune R via the Cayley transform to further smooth activations.
Stage 2 (optional): Permutation Invariant Outlier Grouping (POG)
- Reorder weight-matrix columns to make outlier patterns more amenable to subsequent grouping/clustering, improving robustness to permutations.
Stage 3: Adaptive Weight Clustering with Centroid Finetuning (ACCF)
- Cluster weights into a codebook and fine-tune centroids to minimize output discrepancy, reducing weight-side error even with significant outliers.
Deployment: provide a LUT kernel to execute codebook-based weights efficiently, claiming no extra runtime overhead while improving hardware efficiency.1e:T55b,skim level
- Read the Introduction for the MoE low-precision outlier problem and the motivation for clustering + LUT.
- Look at Figure 1 (3-stage pipeline) and Figure 2 (how rotation is inserted into MoE/FFN and why invariance holds).
- Skim the Methodology headings to map modules: AOS / ACCF / POG / LUT kernel.

  - ablations: rotation only, clustering only, rotation+clustering, with/without POG, with/without centroid fine-tuning;
  - check whether residual errors concentrate in specific layers/experts/tasks.1f:T5e6,HoPA (High-order Proxy Alignment) replaces exhaustive pairwise cross-modal alignment with a compact proxy that captures high-order relationships among all modalities, and plugs this proxy into a trajectory-matching-compatible distillation pipeline.

1) Theoretical motivation (endpoint discrepancy bound): the paper identifies an overlooked factor that upper-bounds teacher–student endpoint discrepancy in the omnimodal setting. As the modality count increases, additional mismatch terms appear, making omnimodal trajectory matching harder under pairwise objectives.

2) High-order proxy alignment: HoPA constructs a compact proxy via multimodal similarity reconstruction and spectral analysis. Intuitively, it extracts the dominant global alignment structure (a low-rank/spectral subspace) from the multimodal similarity relations, so alignment is enforced holistically rather than by enumerating all modality pairs (avoiding O(M^2) blow-up).

3) Compatibility with trajectory matching: the proxy is integrated into standard distillation/trajectory matching—teacher trajectories are recorded on real data; synthetic data are optimized so the student’s trajectory/endpoint matches the teacher while respecting the proxy-based high-order alignment.

4) Spectral selectivity analysis: the paper argues that the spectral proxy better preserves the principal alignment structure than pairwise objectives, yielding a tighter trajectory/endpoint discrepancy bound and improved stability as modalities increase.20:T43a,DOP-OBC is a training-free, architecture-agnostic decoding strategy that modifies attention computation to enforce equitable (not merely equal) representational opportunity.

1) Upper-triangular attention register: add an upper-triangular, linearly decayed matrix P to the attention logits as a reservoir that absorbs surplus mass directed to future positions, mitigating attention collapse while preserving autoregressive causality via careful masking.

2) Progressive positional decay: a diminishing masking/decay schedule introduces absolute positional focus and stabilizes long-range grounding.

3) Two object-aware controls:
- Dominant Object Penalty (DOP): suppresses over-attended objects by reducing row-wise logit amplitude for dominant regions.
- Outlier Boost Coefficient (OBC): boosts rare but confidently detected objects via calibrated scaling, improving long-tail object representation.

Together, DOP reduces head-object monopolization and OBC compensates for structurally under-attended objects, all as plug-in logit/attention modulations without parameter updates.21:T44c,AF-Next follows the Audio Flamingo modular design: a Whisper-family audio encoder (AF-Whisper, further pretrained on larger/more diverse multilingual and multi-talker corpora) + an audio projector into the LLM embedding space + a text-only pretrained LLM backbone + streaming TTS. Audio is resampled to 16 kHz mono, converted to 128-bin log-mel (25 ms window / 10 ms hop), and processed in non-overlapping 30-second chunks with sliding-window style feature extraction, enabling up to ~30 minutes of audio. Training emphasizes scalable internet-scale data curation and post-training for reasoning via a four-stage curriculum with multiple rounds of SFT and GRPO-based RL over carefully curated mixtures. The key new paradigm is Temporal Audio Chain-of-Thought: intermediate reasoning steps are explicitly grounded to timestamps in long audio, enabling fine-grained temporal alignment and traceable evidence for long-form audio reasoning. The release includes three checkpoints: AF-Next-Instruct (general instruction/QA), AF-Next-Think (stronger reasoning), and AF-Next-Captioner (detailed captioning).22:T5d7,must_read (deep dive)
1) Introduction: focus on the critique of benchmark-centric training and the motivation for real-world long-audio robustness and openness.
2) Methodology (incl. Fig. 3): study the end-to-end architecture and long-audio processing (30s chunks, temporal resolution, projection into LLM space) to understand how 30-minute inputs are supported.
3) Data curation + training curriculum: prioritize the internet-scale data construction/filters/mixtures and the four-stage SFT+GRPO RL recipe; map each stage to capability gains (ASR, captioning, instruction following, reasoning).
4) Temporal Audio Chain-of-Thought: treat as the main innovation—read the output format (steps + timestamps), how supervision is built, and where it helps/fails on long-audio tasks.
5) Main results tables and long/real-world settings: concentrate on long/noisy/multi-speaker evaluations and ablations (is scaling alone enough? does timestamped CoT improve alignment and interpretability?).

- Finally skim the Temporal Audio CoT section and qualitative examples to build intuition for timestamp-grounded reasoning outputs.23:T552,STORM (Spatial–Temporal Object Referential Model) is proposed as the first end-to-end MLLM for RMOT that directly generates multi-object bounding boxes without external detectors/trackers.
1) Unified end-to-end design: a ViT-style visual encoder extracts per-frame spatial features; the LLM conditions on these features plus the query to reason over appearance, motion, and language, and outputs bounding boxes in a structured plain-text format (casting localization/tracking as sequence generation).
2) Data-efficient training via TCL (Task-Composition Learning): decompose RMOT into two data-rich subtasks—image referring grounding (language-region alignment) and single-object tracking (temporal consistency/identity). Pretrain on large grounding + SOT datasets, then fine-tune on limited RMOT data with reasoning-oriented supervision that teaches the model to identify all referred objects in the first frame and track them over time.
3) STORM-Bench dataset: built to address small scale, noise, ambiguity, and limited diversity in existing RMOT datasets. A bottom-up annotation pipeline first creates/validates object-level descriptions, then composes multi-object expressions via controlled language reasoning to ensure coverage of attributes, spatial relations, and temporal interactions; reported scale is ~0.2M expressions and 73.7K tracked objects.24:T54c,must_read (deep technical reading)

skim (fast reading)
- Read contributions in the Introduction, the method overview figure (if any), the TCL paragraph, the STORM-Bench paragraph, and the main RMOT results table to capture the core “end-to-end box generation + task composition learning + new benchmark” story.25:T4b0,TraversalBench is a controlled benchmark where each instance contains (1) a single continuous polyline, (2) a unique start marker, and (3) markers placed at path vertices; the task is to output the ordered marker sequence encountered from start to finish. Difficulty is explicitly balanced along four interpretable axes:
1) Self-intersection count, discretized by thresholds {0,1,2,4,6,9,13} with finer granularity near zero crossings.
2) Tortuosity, discretized by thresholds {1.0,1.3,2.0,3.0,4.5,6.5,9.0} into six bins spanning near-straight to highly meandering paths.
3) Vertex count (sequence length / path length), with multiple point-count settings to separate length effects.
4) Confounding lines: nearby non-target segments that increase distractor structure without altering the target path.
Generation targets cells in the joint tortuosity×intersection grid, then applies render-aware acceptance constraints (segment length, nonlocal clearance, vertex-to-segment separation) so difficulty is driven by the intended structural factors rather than rendering artifacts. The final dataset is balanced across feasible complexity cells (some cells are unreachable due to geometric constraints).26:T547,The paper introduces the V2S task and a JSON-style hierarchical output schema. Given an untrimmed cinematic video V, the model predicts Ŷ={M̂,Ŝ}:
- Meta-level M̂: global attributes (title/description, total duration, character list).
- Scene-level Ŝ: an ordered scene sequence {s_i}, each with scene id, location, and coarse time attribute (e.g., day/night).
- Event-level Ê_i: within each scene, ordered events {e_{i,j}} with timestamps and character identity; each event includes at least one of {dialogue, action, expression, audio cue (sound/BGM)}.
The learning objective jointly targets (1) temporal localization (when), (2) character-consistent semantic parsing (who), and (3) multimodal narrative faithfulness (how).

Data/training/model:

- Proposes OmniScript, an 8B omni-modal (audio+visual) script generation model.

- Motivates efficiency concerns: even ~2 minutes can require ~4k tokens, so long-form generation demands careful structuring and training.27:T4eb,must_read (deep technical + how to read):

skim (high-level + what to look at):
- Read the introduction for the three bottlenecks (data, evaluation, token cost) and the three contributions.
- Skim the schema section and one output example (if provided) to understand what “script” means here.
- Jump to the main results table to see the performance gap and where OmniScript sits relative to strong closed models.28:T42b,MTSS (Multi-Stream Scene Script) replaces monolithic captions with a grounded, editable scene-script built on two principles:
1) Stream Factorization: represent a video with four complementary streams—Reference (persistent entity/scene bank with stable IDs and appearance anchors), Shot (temporally bounded visual segments), Event (localized audio events/utterances), and Global (shared ambient context). This separates persistent information from time-varying content to reduce redundancy and enable local edits.
2) Relational Grounding: reconnect the factorized streams via explicit identity and temporal links. Identity grounding uses a centralized reference bank so Shot/Event entries can consistently refer to the same entities across shots. Temporal grounding aligns visual segments with concurrent audio events via shared anchors, and uses intra-description timestamps for finer action–speech–scene correspondences.
Net effect: a cohesive script that preserves cross-modal relations while remaining modular and scalable for refinement and downstream use.29:T4ef,【skim】
- Read the Introduction’s critique of monolithic captions and Figure 1 for the key intuition (local edits vs global rewrites; smaller-model benefits).
- Use Figure 2 (overall MTSS structure) and Figure 3 (script example) to quickly understand what the representation looks like.
- Skim the results focusing on three buckets: captioning (TER/detail), reasoning (Daily-Omni/WorldSense), and generation (human-rated multi-shot metrics).

- Key novelty to track: treating attention as an intervenable variable and using a push–pull formulation to bypass safety retrieval, thereby reducing gradient conflict and improving attack efficiency.2b:T461,Introduce LARYBench, a unified benchmark that decouples latent-action evaluation into two complementary dimensions and instantiates them as standardized tasks: (1) High-level semantic intent (“what to do”) via Semantic Action Classification over atomic and composite actions; (2) Low-level physical controllability (“how to do it”) via Low-level Control Regression that tests whether representations preserve enough dynamics to reconstruct end-effector trajectories. To enable this, the authors build an automated data engine to re-segment and re-annotate heterogeneous embodied video sources, producing a large, unified corpus (1.2M short videos, 620K image pairs, 595K trajectories; 151 action categories; 11 robot embodiments; human/robot agents; egocentric/exocentric; sim/real). They benchmark three model families: Embodied LAMs, off-the-shelf General Vision Encoders (semantic-level and pixel-level), and proposed General LAMs created by grafting LAM training onto frozen general vision backbones (e.g., LAPA-DINOv3, LAPA-SigLIP2), isolating the effect of visual priors vs latent-action training paradigms.2c:T4d9,[Skim] Read: Introduction (motivation: evaluation decoupling + what/how split), Benchmark/Task definitions (what exactly is measured), Experiments (11-model comparison and the main ranking), Conclusion (implications for future VLA training).
[Must-read] Focus on: (1) the automated data engine and dataset composition (how re-segmentation/re-annotation addresses noisy temporal boundaries and inconsistent labels; how coverage enables generalization claims); (2) formal task setups and metrics (how inputs/outputs are aligned so evaluation targets representation rather than policy); (3) model-family design and controls (Embodied LAM vs General Encoder vs General LAM; freezing strategy; tokenization/latent units); (4) the main result tables/figures contrasting semantic classification vs control regression and semantic-level vs pixel-level backbones, plus any error analysis that diagnoses missing semantics vs missing dynamics.
[Innovation hooks] (a) first standardized, representation-level benchmark for latent actions decoupled from policy; (b) joint evaluation of semantic and control granularity; (c) “frozen backbone + LAM training” as a critical ablation axis to test whether the latent-action paradigm itself helps or hurts.2d:T59e,The paper proposes EmergentBridge, a lightweight embedding-level bridging framework that can be applied on top of a pretrained unified embedding model. Instead of aligning a new modality Mb only to an anchor embedding C, EmergentBridge synthesizes a proxy embedding for an already-aligned modality Ma from the anchor embedding (i.e., a predicted/constructed target \hat{x}^a derived from c). Training then aligns Mb jointly to (1) the anchor c and (2) the synthesized proxy \hat{x}^a, thereby strengthening Mb↔Ma relations without requiring paired (Mb,Ma) data.

The method is motivated via an InfoNCE analysis: the loss is decomposed into a positive alignment term and a negative log-partition term, and the gradient w.r.t. the modality embedding provides a geometric notion of the “anchor-alignment direction” (for cosine similarity with L2-normalized embeddings, it aligns with the anchor direction up to scaling/projection). Since proxy signals can be noisy and may interfere with anchor alignment, the paper introduces an orthogonal-subspace alignment regularizer: it constrains the additional proxy-driven alignment to lie in the subspace orthogonal to the anchor-alignment direction, using remaining degrees of freedom to absorb proxy constraints while preserving anchor performance. The paper further discusses theoretical conditions under which anchor-modality alignment is preserved under this subspace-constrained update.2e:T44a,must_read (deep dive):
1) Introduction: understand the failure mode “anchor-aligned but non-anchor-misaligned” and the definition/measurement of emergent alignment.
2) Preliminaries: focus on the InfoNCE decomposition and the gradient-based geometric interpretation of the anchor-alignment direction.
3) Method: (i) how proxy embeddings are synthesized from anchor embeddings and used as extra targets; (ii) how the orthogonal-subspace regularizer prevents proxy noise from harming anchor alignment.
4) Theory/conditions: read the preservation argument for anchor alignment (subspace/gradient decomposition intuition).
5) Experiments: prioritize main tables for unpaired-pair retrieval/classification, anchor-pair retention, and ablations (with/without orthogonal constraint, proxy noise sensitivity, bridge strength).

skim (quick read):
1) Read Introduction + contributions for the big picture.
2) Skim Preliminaries just enough to grasp why orthogonal subspace matters.
3) Jump to main results and anchor-retention comparisons; briefly scan ablations to confirm which components drive gains.2f:T738,(must_read: detailed technical analysis)
1) Architecture: CSLM = speech tokenizer + joint speech-text LLM + speech decoder. Speech is discretized using CosyVoice-300M-25hz tokens (4096 vocab, 25Hz). Consecutive repeated speech tokens are merged before feeding into the LLM to shorten sequences and improve efficiency (claimed to be mostly acoustic redundancy without semantic loss). The LLM merges the speech-token vocabulary with the text vocabulary to perform unified autoregressive modeling over both token types. The decoder uses a duration predictor to expand the merged token sequence back to a duration-aware sequence, then a conditional flow matching model to generate mel-spectrograms, and HiFi-GAN to synthesize waveform.
2) Key training idea: continual pretraining with a novel alignment strategy to achieve cross-modal (speech↔text) and cross-lingual (lang A↔lang B) alignment simultaneously under limited data. Discrete speech tokens serve as the bridge to place speech and text into a unified token space, while the alignment strategy injects signals during continual pretraining to learn transferable mappings across modalities and languages.
3) Instruction tuning: speech-text interleaved chain-of-modality generation. The model is fine-tuned to generate through interleaved modality steps (alternating speech/text segments), strengthening alignment at finer granularity, improving generation quality, and reducing latency (via more efficient generation paths and shorter/cleaner speech-token sequences).

(skim: method overview)
- Treat discretized speech tokens as first-class tokens inside an LLM by merging vocabularies;
- Use continual pretraining to align both modalities and languages;
- Use interleaved speech/text chain-of-modality SFT to further refine alignment, improve conversational generation, and reduce latency.30:T57f,must_read
- Key sections: Introduction (problem framing and differentiation vs SPIRIT LM/Moshi/GLM-4-Voice), Model: CSLM (token merging, vocab merging, duration predictor + flow-matching decoding), Training procedure (details of the continual-pretraining alignment strategy), Instruction fine-tuning (exact interleaved chain-of-modality format and why it reduces latency).
- Key figures/tables to focus on (if present): overall training pipeline; examples of interleaved modality chains; main cross-modal/cross-lingual results; ablations (w/ or w/o alignment strategy, interleaved SFT, token merging, duration predictor) and quality–latency trade-off plots.
- Innovation checklist: (i) how the alignment signals are constructed to jointly align modality and language; (ii) where the gains of interleaved chain-of-modality SFT come from (alignment granularity, exposure bias, token length, decoding stability); (iii) how token merging + duration prediction affect intelligibility, prosody, and latency.

skim
- Read the three bullet contributions at the end of the Introduction;
- Skim the CSLM components and the efficiency design (repeat-token merging + duration predictor);
- Jump to the main result tables and latency comparisons to confirm robustness under limited speech data;
- Briefly scan the comparison discussion vs SPIRIT LM/Moshi/GLM-4-Voice to judge portability to your target languages/data.31:T6ac,(must_read: detailed technical analysis)
1) Physics Evaluator: a unified evaluator covering four aspects—Geometric Priors, Contact, Stability, and Deployability—further decomposed into nine measurable constraints. A key challenge is differentiability: Contact and Stability are (partly) differentiable, while Geometric Priors and Deployability are inherently non-differentiable.
2) PhyMix framework: augments diffusion-based scene generation backbones with evaluator-driven implicit and explicit optimization.
- Implicit optimization (training): Scene-GRPO (Scene-level Group Relative Policy Optimization). Inspired by preference alignment in LLMs, the model samples multiple candidate layouts per input, ranks them using evaluator scores, and optimizes the generator to assign higher probability to physically feasible layouts—thereby injecting especially non-differentiable constraints into the sampling distribution.
- Explicit optimization (inference): TTO (Test-Time Optimizer). At test time, it explicitly refines object poses using differentiable physical constraints (primarily contact/stability) to fix remaining violations.
3) Closed-loop design: the evaluator serves as both the benchmark and the guidance signal; implicit optimization handles non-differentiable constraints at the distribution level, while explicit optimization performs continuous refinement for differentiable constraints.

(skim: method overview)
- Build a comprehensive physics benchmark (4 categories, 9 constraints);
- Train with evaluator-ranked samples via GRPO-style preference optimization to handle non-differentiable constraints;
- Refine at inference with differentiable constraints via test-time optimization.32:T4e4,must_read
- Key sections: Introduction; Physics Evaluator (formal definitions of 4 aspects/9 constraints, differentiable vs non-differentiable split, correlation with human judgments); Scene-GRPO (how evaluator scores become preference signals and how group-relative advantages are formed); TTO (objective, gradients, convergence/speed); Experiments + ablations (remove GRPO/TTO/constraint groups).
- Key figures/tables: qualitative failure-case comparisons (collision/floating/instability/navigability), per-constraint breakdown (tables or radar plots), human-alignment plots, main physics-score improvement table, and visual-fidelity metrics to confirm no regression.
- Innovation checklist: (i) stability of the evaluator→preference-training loop and its effect on diversity; (ii) effectiveness/limits of injecting non-differentiable constraints via preference learning; (iii) whether TTO introduces local minima or harms visual consistency and how trade-offs are managed.

- Skim the overall PhyMix diagram to understand GRPO (train) vs TTO (test);
- Jump to main physics-score tables and qualitative examples;
- Glance at ablations to see which constraints/stages drive gains.33:T714,(must_read level: detailed technical analysis)
1) Representation & Tokenizer: The paper adopts Lottie (JSON) as the target format and proposes the first Lottie Tokenizer. It decomposes a Lottie file into semantically aligned tokens covering geometric primitives, hierarchical grouping/layers, parametric transforms, and time-dependent animation properties (keyframes) including interpolation/easing. A central design choice is to encode keyframes and easing functions rather than dense per-frame samples, greatly reducing sequence length while preserving renderable structural fidelity, and unifying hierarchy + temporal motion into a single autoregressive stream.
2) Data: The authors build Lottie-660K, the first large-scale vector animation corpus (660K animations) exported from After Effects via the Bodymovin pipeline. They also curate 15M static vector graphics converted into Lottie to enable a progressive “static-first, then dynamic” training strategy. A rigorous cleaning/standardization pipeline filters rendering failures and anomalies (blank frames, misalignment, flicker) and simplifies JSON by removing redundant fields/metadata, reducing sequence length by ~34% without quality loss.
3) Model: They finetune Qwen2.5-VL into LottieGPT, a multimodal autoregressive model that generates fully editable Lottie animations from text, images, or keyframes (i.e., outputs structured JSON programs). For multimodal supervision, BLIP-2 captions static graphics; animations are rendered to video and described with temporally aligned captions using Qwen2.5-VL 32B.
4) Evaluation: They introduce LottieBench as the first benchmark for vector animation and report new SOTA on SVG generation (treated as a single-frame special case), suggesting temporal modeling can also improve static vector generation.34:T5db,(must_read level: detailed technical analysis)
1) Motivation: FM learns the marginal velocity field of a probability flow by minimizing an L2/MSE loss between predicted and target conditional velocities. While infinite-capacity models can recover the true flow, finite-capacity models trained under a fixed Euclidean metric may generalize off the data manifold, yielding OOD samples. Using a fixed perceptual network as the metric can be exploited (“generator hacking”), motivating joint training of the criterion.
2) Method: Continuous Adversarial Flow Models (CAFMs) introduce adversarial training into continuous-time flow modeling. Unlike discrete-time Adversarial Flow Models (AFMs), CAFMs operate in continuous time (ODE/CNF) and replace the fixed MSE criterion with a learned discriminator to guide learning. This changes the effective generalized distribution induced under finite capacity and empirically yields samples closer to the target distribution.
3) Training usage: Primarily proposed as post-training (finetuning) of existing FM models for efficiency, though it can also train from scratch. The paper emphasizes FM and CAFM target the same ground-truth flow; differences arise mainly from finite-capacity generalization under different objectives.
4) Intuition: Discriminators are more sensitive to perceptual details (texture, sharpness, contours) than pointwise L2 losses and may better capture manifold structure; jointly learned criteria mitigate fixed-metric exploitation.35:T434,(must_read: deep reading)
- Read Introduction to understand the claimed root cause (Euclidean metric → manifold-mismatched generalization) and positioning vs Riemannian FM, perceptual losses, GANs, and AFMs.
- Read Background carefully (FM derivations): conditional vs marginal velocity and why the FM objective depends on properties of the distance/criterion—this is the theoretical hook for swapping MSE with a learned discriminator.
- Read the CAFM method section in detail: how the adversarial objective is defined in continuous time/ODE, what the discriminator sees, how time t is incorporated, stability/regularization tricks, and the key differences from discrete-time AFM.
- Read experiments focusing on the post-training protocol (epochs, optimizer, discriminator architecture/regularization, sampler changes) and compare guidance-free vs guided to judge whether gains are true distribution alignment vs mere sharpening.
(skim: quick reading)
- For takeaways only: read Introduction + the main ImageNet FID table (SiT/JiT) + the text-to-image GenEval/DPG results.36:T504,The paper introduces RationalRewards, a reasoning-based reward model that outputs structured, multi-dimensional critiques—covering text faithfulness, physical/visual quality, text rendering, and (for editing) source-image faithfulness—before producing scores/preferences.
To train such a model without labeled rationales, it proposes PARROT (Preference-Anchored Rationalization). PARROT treats the rationale z as a latent variable and learns a student evaluator Pθ(z,y|x) from pairwise preference tuples x=(IA,IB,c) with label y. A variational ELBO yields a teacher–student training recipe (Fig.4): (1) a teacher VLM generates candidate rationales anchored to the known preference y (posterior / hindsight qϕ(z|x,y)); (2) a consistency filter removes hallucinated or non-predictive rationales, keeping those that are sufficient to predict y; (3) a student is distilled to generate rationales (without seeing y) and predict preference/score.
RationalRewards is then used in two complementary optimization spaces: (a) parameter space—structured critiques yield interpretable, fine-grained RL rewards; (b) prompt space—at test time, a Generate–Critique–Refine loop turns critiques into targeted prompt revisions, trading compute for quality without any parameter updates.37:T69e,must_read (deep technical + how to read)
- Read the Introduction for the central shift: scalar black-box rewards → structured reasoning rewards, and the dual benefits in parameter space (training) and prompt space (inference). Use Fig.3 to internalize why scalar rewards invite reward hacking.
- Study the Method section’s ELBO formulation: x=(IA,IB,c), preference y, latent rationale z. Understand the posterior (hindsight) vs prior (foresight) roles and how they map to teacher vs student.
- Focus on Fig.4: the theory-to-practice pipeline—anchored rationale generation → consistency filtering → distillation. Pay attention to what “predictive sufficiency/consistency” means operationally and how it mitigates hallucinated rationales.
- Examine Table 1 (preference prediction) and Tables 2–3 (RL improvements), and track gains across critique dimensions (faithfulness, plausibility, text rendering, edit faithfulness).
- Understand Generate–Critique–Refine and why it differs from pre-generation prompt rewriting: it is post-hoc and reactive, using critiques of the actual output to revise prompts.
- Key contributions to extract: (1) variational latent-rationale training from preferences (PARROT), (2) multi-dimensional structured critiques as a reward interface, (3) test-time critique-driven prompt refinement.

skim (high-level + what to look at)
- Read the Introduction for problem framing and the two optimization spaces.
- Jump to Fig.4 to grasp PARROT’s pipeline quickly.
- Skim the main result tables (Table 1, Tables 2–3) and the section describing Generate–Critique–Refine to capture the headline finding: strong test-time gains without weight updates.38:T64d,must_read (deep technical + how to read)
- Read the Introduction’s two fundamental issues carefully (redundancy and spatial inconsistency) and use Fig.2’s token-count example to quantify the inefficiency of 2D multi-view latents.
- Study Fig.1 to understand the full system: 3DRAE learns 3D tokens; 3DDiT performs diffusion in that 3D latent; decoding renders views.
- In Method, focus on the “Autoencoders for Diffusion” setup: why standard 2D VAEs are considered low-capacity and reconstruction-biased, motivating a more semantic, generation-suitable latent—and then map that motivation to 3DRAE’s design goals (fixed-length, 3D-aware, novel-view capable, optional point-map prediction).

skim (high-level + what to look at)
- Read the first part of the Introduction plus Fig.1 and Fig.2.
- Keep only the two-module picture: 3DRAE = multi-view → fixed 3D tokens; 3DDiT = diffusion over 3D tokens.
- If time is limited, jump to the main experiment figures/tables about token/compute savings and long-trajectory consistency (typically in the experiments section).39:T51f,The paper proposes Cascading Low-rank Fitting: instead of using isolated LoRA adapters for each derivative order i, it sequentially accumulates all low-rank components up to order i into the effective weights, and evaluates the i-th derivative mapping using the same base function f on these accumulated weights. Concretely, the i-th order uses f((W-\sum_{j\le i}A_jB_j)x) rather than f((W-A_iB_i)x), aligning with an ODE view where higher-order states depend on lower-order states.

For analysis, the paper focuses on linear weight transformations and defines L_i(t)=d^{i-1}W(t)/dt^{i-1} - d^{i}W(t)/dt^{i}. It introduces r-linear decomposability for matrix functions: U(t)=\sum_{j=1}^r f_j(t) u_j v_j^\top with u_j,v_j independent of t. Under this assumption, it proves a monotone rank property: if L_1(t) is r1-linearly decomposable with rank r1, then ranks satisfy r1\ge r2\ge...\ge rk. This provides theoretical support for allocating progressively smaller LoRA ranks for higher-order dynamics.

The authors also argue the framework can extend to attention softmax via polynomial approximation techniques, enabling similar low-rank/cascaded approximations for the quadratic pairwise correlations in modern attention (the provided content emphasizes feasibility and connection rather than full derivations).3a:T457,Pipeline:
1) Controlled synthetic datasets: for each attribute, vary only that attribute while keeping others relatively fixed; create multiple splits.
2) LoRA fine-tune per split: obtain LoRA updates dW_i=A_iB_i^T for each split.
3) Shared subspace extraction: assume each dW_i contains a shared (attribute) component plus split-specific noise. Solve a robust generalized least-squares objective to fit all dW_i with a common low-dimensional factor AB^T:
   - min_{A,B} \sum_i ||A_iB_i^T-AB^T||_F^\alpha (\alpha improves robustness to outliers).
   - Use IRLS: alternate between updating weights w_i and updating (A,B).
   - With fixed w_i, form C=\sum_i w_i A_iB_i^T and compute truncated SVD C=U\Sigma V^T; set A=U_{d'}\Sigma_{d'}^{1/2}, B=V_{d'}\Sigma_{d'}^{1/2}.

After obtaining a shared subspace per attribute, the paper measures cross-attribute orthogonality (disentanglement). It then concatenates/aggregates attribute subspaces and applies PCA to form a compact “LoRA basis”. Downstream fine-tuning is performed within this basis via dW=\bar A M \bar B^T, where only the small matrix M is trained.3b:T5d9,1) Degeneration analysis (Triviality Bias): with confidence reward r_e(y_i|s_i)=log π_s(y_i|s_i), the one-step objective E_{y~π}[r_e] is linear in π(·|s), so the maximizer is an extreme point of the simplex—i.e., a deterministic distribution selecting the highest-probability token under π_s. This drives low-entropy behavior; in writing, it manifests as high-frequency, formulaic phrasing.

2) Relative information gain decomposition: define a generalist reference policy π_b (pre-finetune) and a specialist policy π_s (SFT on high-quality in-domain data). For each token: log π_s = log π_b + log(π_s/π_b). The log-likelihood ratio ϕ(y_i|s_i)=log(π_s/π_b) captures token-level domain-specific information beyond generic high-frequency preference.

3) TCER reward: directly using log-ratio can be numerically unstable when π_b is tiny, so TCER keeps log π_s as the base reward and adds a gated triviality correction: r_TCER = log π_s(y_i|s_i) + w(y_i|s_i)·ϕ_smooth(y_i|s_i). The gate uses probability-dependent suppression (1-π_s(y_i|s_i))^λ scaled by k, strongly down-weighting very high-probability tokens and reallocating reward to informative yet less predictable choices. ε-smoothing is applied to bound and stabilize the log-ratio.

4) Optimization: the model is trained with Reference Augmented GRPO (group relative policy optimization with reference augmentation), combining TCER’s token-level signal with reference-based regularization for stable judge-free RFT.3c:T485,must_read (deep technical + what to focus on):
- Introduction: problem framing (no verifiable reward in writing) and the key empirical finding (confidence rewards help but degenerate), plus the definition of Triviality Bias.
- Methodology: carefully read the degeneration proof sketch (linearity → extreme point → low entropy). This is the paper’s key “why it fails”.
- Figures/Formulas: Figure 1 (improvement vs degeneration), Figure 2 (full framework), and the TCER equations: log-ratio ϕ, gate (1-π_s)^λ, and ε-smoothing/boundedness discussion.
- Innovations to scrutinize:
  1) Specialist-vs-generalist relative information gain to isolate domain-specific signals;
  2) Probability-gated suppression to explicitly counter entropy collapse;
  3) Integration with GRPO + reference augmentation for practical training.

skim (method overview + what to read):
- Read Introduction + Figure 1 to get the core phenomenon.
- Read the TCER definition paragraph (log π_s + gated log-ratio) and Figure 2 for the pipeline.
- Skim main result tables and ablations to confirm the quality/diversity trade-off improvements over vanilla confidence reward.3d:T55b,1) Hindsight process rewards: define a dense process reward r_P(s_t,a_t) that can depend on the future suffix trajectory τ_{>t} (hindsight), so actions that are strategically useful but not immediately rewarded can receive credit. The overall objective combines sparse extrinsic rewards r_E with dense r_P.

2) Proxy reward policy via Gibbs form: represent r_P with a proxy policy π_P(a_t|τ_{<t},τ_{>t}) ∝ π_ref(a_t|τ_{<t})·exp(β r_P). The paper proves that minimizing KL(π_θ || π_P) is equivalent to maximizing a KL-regularized process-reward objective (details in the appendix).

3) Mutual-information interpretation (core theory): expanding KL(π_θ || π_P) yields (i) a conditional mutual information term I(a_t; τ_{>t} | τ_{<t}), capturing dependence between the action and the future trajectory, and (ii) a KL regularization term to a reference/proxy distribution. The authors interpret optimizing hindsight self-evaluation rewards through this MI+KL lens (as derived in the paper).

4) MISE paradigm: Mutual Information Self-Evaluation integrates dense self-evaluation rewards with sparse environmental rewards while calibrating the self-evaluation against environmental feedback to avoid systematic biases (e.g., inventory over-positivity). Training improves both the action policy and the self-evaluation component, yielding mutual reinforcement.3e:T490,must_read (deep technical + what to focus on):
- Introduction: motivation (reward sparsity) and the concrete bias failure case (inventory), to understand why calibration is essential.
- Method: carefully read the Gibbs-form proxy policy and the KL-equivalence derivation (cross-check Appendix A). This is the key bridge from self-evaluation to a principled RL objective.
- The MI decomposition where I(a;τ_{>t}|τ_{<t}) appears: this is the main theoretical contribution; align symbols with the trajectory definitions.
- Calibration mechanism: focus on how environmental feedback is used to correct self-evaluation bias (implementation details, losses, update flow).
- Experiments/ablations: prioritize “no calibration vs calibration” comparisons, including behavior distribution shifts (inventory rate) and final performance gains.

skim (method overview + what to read):

- Skim the Method section for the MISE pipeline (diagram/pseudocode if present) and the proxy policy definition.
- Jump to main results and key ablations (self-reward, calibration, vs extrinsic-only) to see where gains come from.3f:T44a,It introduces the Four Quadrant Decomposition, factorizing token updates by (i) reward polarity (from positive vs. negative trajectories) and (ii) token entropy (high vs. low predictive uncertainty under the policy). Controlled ablations show reasoning improvements concentrate in the high-entropy quadrants. Theoretically, the authors adapt Conditional Mutual Information (CMI) to the autoregressive RLVR setting to formalize token–reward dependence and prove that the plausible credit a token can carry is upper-bounded by its entropy. A complementary GRPO gradient analysis explains how uniform reward broadcast misallocates or dilutes learning signal across entropy regimes.
Based on this, they propose Entropy-Aware Policy Optimization (EAPO): a minimal instantiation that scales token-level learning signals by token entropy (without hard thresholds), concentrating updates where credit is most likely. They also articulate distinct roles at high entropy: positive updates reinforce correct branches (exploration/generalization), while negative updates prune erroneous branches (precision).40:T40c,must_read (deep dive):
1) Figure 1 + Four Quadrant Decomposition: the paper’s core diagnostic coordinate system—understand the polarity×entropy partition and the controlled ablation protocol.
2) CMI adaptation and the “credit ≤ entropy” bound: track variable definitions under autoregressive conditioning and the key inequality/intuition that yields testable predictions.
3) GRPO gradient analysis: see precisely how uniform broadcast interacts with entropy to shape per-token gradient contributions.
4) EAPO formulation: how entropy enters the token-level surrogate/advantage; whether it requires extra models or only policy probabilities.
5) Experiments: prioritize quadrant ablations (diagnosis) and OOD / cross-family results (generalization).
